references.notes: The verse explicitly mentions a 'ghoul' (غُول), a type of supernatural being in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'al-maniyya' (المَنيَّة) refers to fate or death as an irresistible cosmological force, a common concept in pre-Islamic thought.
The poet swears an oath by 'the Lord of Mecca' (a pre-Islamic sacred site) and 'the Cross', reflecting the presence of both traditional Arabian reverence and Christianity in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The term 'الشعوب' refers to a pre-Islamic concept of the paths to the underworld or the gathering place of souls after death.
The phrase 'يسار الله' (God's help/ease) is an invocation of divine assistance, consistent with the monotheistic beliefs (Christianity) present in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The poet entrusts his affairs to 'a Lord who is near and responsive', which is an invocation of a monotheistic deity, reflecting Christian belief in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The mention of a 'raven falling' (غُراب... واقِعا) refers to the pre-Islamic practice of augury (ṭīra), where the actions of birds like ravens were interpreted as omens.
The verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) and 'rayb al-manūn' (the vicissitudes of fate/death) as forces that afflict people, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological views of Dahr as an active agent.
The term 'مغوَل' (maghwal) refers to a ghoul or a type of jinn, which is a common supernatural entity in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse contains an oath sworn by "Dhāt al-Wadaʿ" (Mistress of the Cowries), a known pre-Islamic deity, indicating a direct reference to a pagan belief.
The phrase "لأمر دهر" refers to "Dahr" (Time/Fate) as a powerful, often malevolent, cosmological force in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'al-Manūn' refers to fate or death as an irresistible, often personified, cosmological force in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase 'al-Hadī ʿinda al-Āl' refers to sacrificial animals offered at or to idols/sacred stones, indicating a pre-Islamic ritual practice.
The term 'qidaḥayka' (your two arrows) explicitly refers to the arrows used in pre-Islamic divination (azlām).
This verse contains an oath sworn by an unspecified divine entity, which is a common form of oath in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This verse contains an explicit oath sworn by 'Allāh,' a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabian society, even among polytheists.
The term 'haykal' refers to a temple or sacred building, representing a sacred space relevant to pre-Islamic religious practices.
The poem repeatedly refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a powerful, destructive, and often personified cosmological force, a central theme in pre-Islamic fatalism.
The term 'maniyya' (fate/death) is used to describe an irresistible force that snatches away life, aligning with pre-Islamic concepts of fate.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) is used to describe the relentless and inevitable forces of death, a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an active agent that 'does not recover,' reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of Dahr as an impersonal, irresistible force of fate.
The term 'أَصِيص' refers to a small idol or image, and 'شَرباً حَوالي أَصِيص' describes a gathering and drinking around such a sacred object, indicating a pre-Islamic ritual practice.

The verses refer to 'Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an active, powerful, and destructive cosmological force that brings calamities, humbles people, destroys mountains, and casts individuals through life, reflecting a pre-Islamic fatalistic worldview.

The phrase 'معقد الميثاق' refers to the binding nature of a sacred oath or covenant, which in pre-Islamic Arabia was often understood to be sanctioned by divine or sacred powers, and breaking it (حنث) was a serious transgression.
The term 'الحرام' refers to a sacred, inviolable precinct or sanctuary, a key concept in pre-Islamic Arabian sacred geography and ritual practice.
The verse explicitly refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a force that brings misfortune, a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verse questions if 'al-Manūn' (Death/Fates) grants immortality, referencing a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept of inescapable fate.
The phrase 'rayb al-Manūn' (the vicissitudes of Death/Fates) explicitly refers to the unpredictable and inescapable power of fate, a core pre-Islamic cosmological belief.
The mention of 'nawʾ min al-dalw' refers to the pre-Islamic Arabian belief that the setting or rising of specific stars (nawʾ) brings rain or other weather phenomena, which is an astral belief.
The term 'دهري' (my dahr/time/fate) is used here to describe an active agent that turns good and evil, aligning with the pre-Islamic concept of Dahr as a cosmological force governing destiny.
The term "الدَّهر" (time/fate) is invoked as a powerful, destructive cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic belief.
The term "الَمنُونِ" (fate/death) is presented as an inescapable cosmological force, reflecting pre-Islamic fatalistic beliefs.
The explicit mention of "جِنَّ" (jinn) refers to non-divine supernatural entities, a key element of pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This verse refers to 'nawʾ' (star-setting), which was a pre-Islamic astral belief system associating celestial events with weather patterns, specifically rain.
Multiple verses personify and attribute agency to 'الدَّهر' (Time/Fate), presenting it as an active, controlling cosmological force, which is a key pre-Islamic concept.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/destinies) refers to a pre-Islamic cosmological concept of an inescapable, pre-ordained fate.
The phrase 'man tuktab lahu' (whoever is written for him) refers to the pre-ordained nature of destiny, a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term 'maniyyatī' (my fate/death) and the phrase 'ḥummat limīqātin' (it was decreed for a fixed time) explicitly refer to the pre-ordained nature of death, a cosmological concept.
The term 'dahr' (time/fate) is a central pre-Islamic cosmological concept, often personified as an active force that brings about events.
The term 'al-dahr' (time/fate) is again invoked as a cosmological force that reveals truths, consistent with pre-Islamic beliefs.
The mention of 'qiyāmatī' (my death/standing) in conjunction with 'nādibātī wa ʿuwwadī' (my wailing women and visitors) explicitly refers to death and the associated pre-Islamic mourning rites.
This verse explicitly describes the act of wailing and lamentation ('yanujna ʿalā mayyitin wa yuʿlinna rannatan') over the deceased, which is a specific pre-Islamic mortuary practice.
The phrase 'شهراً محرماً' (a sacred month) refers to one of the pre-Islamic sacred months during which fighting was forbidden, indicating a specific ritualistic period.
This verse refers to stars, specifically those of al-ʿAwwā (a lunar mansion), as providing aid, indicating a belief in astral influence on human affairs.
The verse refers to 'Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an active, often malevolent, cosmological force, which is a key pre-Islamic belief.
The verse explicitly mentions 'الصَّنَمِ' (the idol), which refers to a physical object of worship central to pre-Islamic Arabian ritual practice.

This verse contains an oath formula invoking 'the Lord of the Ḥill and the Ḥaram,' which refers to the sacred and profane zones around Mecca, a concept central to pre-Islamic Arabian religious geography and pilgrimage, thus fitting the 'Deities_and_Oaths' category.
The term "المنايا" (al-manāyā) refers to an impersonal, irresistible force of fate or death, a common cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse refers to 'al-manūn' (fate/death) as an active force that changes and destroys, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological views of fate.
The verse explicitly mentions a 'jinnī' (jinn) as the agent of a weapon, directly referencing a pre-Islamic supernatural entity.
The verse refers to 'al-dahr' (time/fate) as a force that bestows or takes away, reflecting the pre-Islamic concept of dahr as an active cosmological agent.
The phrase "كَرَبيبِ البَيتِ" (like the foster-child of the House) refers to a special status associated with the Kaaba, a central pre-Islamic sanctuary.
The term "تَسحيرُ اللَّبَن" (enchanting the milk) refers to a magical practice, which falls under the broader category of divination and magical arts in pre-Islamic belief.
The verse mentions 'yasar' in the context of 'my lot' (خَطِّي), referring to one of the arrows used in the pre-Islamic practice of azlām or maysir for divination or determining shares.
This verse refers to "الدَّهر" (Time/Fate) as an active cosmological force that brings about change or misfortune, a common concept in pre-Islamic Arabian thought.
The term 'muḥriman' refers to the state of iḥrām, a ritual consecration associated with pilgrimage and sacred prohibitions in pre-Islamic Arabian practice.
This verse personifies death as an unstoppable 'driver,' reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of death as an inevitable and active force.

The verse refers to 'الدَّهر' (dahr) as an active force that 'ties the days' and 'is sharpened,' reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of dahr as an impersonal, irresistible force of fate or time.
The verse explicitly mentions 'وثن' (idol), a technical term referring to objects of worship in pre-Islamic Arabian religion, even in the context of denying their presence for God.
The verse attributes change and transformation to "dahr," reflecting the pre-Islamic concept of time or fate as an active cosmological force.

The verse personifies 'Time' (الزَّمَانُ) as an active agent that favors individuals, and uses 'dahr' (دَهراً) in a context that aligns with the pre-Islamic concept of dahr as a cosmological force governing fate and events.
The verse describes the staff (العصا) as conveying information, implying a divinatory or magical function where the object itself is a source of knowledge.
The term "المنايا" (fates/deaths) is presented as an active, irresistible force, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of destiny.
The phrase "ريب الدهر" (the vicissitudes of Time/Fate) refers to "الدهر" as a powerful, often destructive, cosmological force in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'al-manūn' refers to fate or death as an impersonal, irresistible cosmological force, a common concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'ṣafāyā' refers to animals chosen by a chief as his prerogative, a pre-Islamic tribal custom with sacral undertones, fitting the category of ritualized allocation.
The verse attributes agency to the stars ('speaking against us'), suggesting a pre-Islamic astral belief in their influence or observational capacity.
The verses describe the calls of a shrike and a raven, which the speaker interprets as omens of separation and departure, a common form of augury (ṭīra) in pre-Islamic Arabia.
The verse describes the slaughter of an animal and the examination of its entrails, which is a clear reference to the pre-Islamic practice of divination known as haruspicy.
The term "الدَّهر" is used here as an active agent ("turns of Dahr have bent me"), reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of Dahr as an impersonal force of fate or time.
The verse refers to 'al-munūn' (fates/deaths) as an active, irresistible cosmological force bringing about a destined day, characteristic of pre-Islamic concepts of fate.
